title: Newton's Laws of Motion
content: Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia): An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion unless acted upon by an external force. Second Law: Force = Mass × Acceleration (F=ma). More force means more acceleration. Third Law: For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. Example: When you push a wall, the wall pushes you back. These laws form the foundation of classical mechanics.

title: Gravity and Gravitation
content: Gravity is a force of attraction between any two objects with mass. Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation: F = G × (m1 × m2) / r². G = 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N⋅m²/kg². Earth's gravitational acceleration g = 9.8 m/s². Weight = mass × g. Weight changes on different planets but mass remains same. On Moon, gravity is 1/6th of Earth. Gravitational force keeps planets orbiting the Sun.

title: Light and Optics
content: Light travels in straight lines at 3 × 10⁸ m/s in vacuum. Reflection: angle of incidence = angle of reflection. Refraction: bending of light when passing between mediums (Snell's Law). Concave mirror converges light (used in torches, headlights). Convex mirror diverges light (used in rear-view mirrors). Convex lens converges light (used in magnifying glass, cameras). Concave lens diverges light (used in spectacles for myopia). Dispersion: white light splits into 7 colors (VIBGYOR) through a prism.

title: Electricity and Current
content: Electric current is the flow of electrons. Measured in Amperes (A). Voltage (V) is the potential difference that pushes current. Resistance (R) opposes current flow, measured in Ohms (Ω). Ohm's Law: V = I × R. Power = V × I (Watts). Series circuit: same current flows through all components, voltages add up. Parallel circuit: same voltage across all components, currents add up. Conductors allow current (metals), Insulators resist current (rubber, plastic).

title: Sound Waves
content: Sound is a mechanical wave that needs a medium to travel. Speed of sound: in air ~343 m/s, in water ~1480 m/s, in steel ~5960 m/s. Frequency: number of vibrations per second (Hz). Audible range: 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz. Below 20 Hz = infrasound, above 20,000 Hz = ultrasound. Pitch depends on frequency (high frequency = high pitch). Loudness depends on amplitude. Echo occurs when sound reflects off a surface at least 17m away. Sonar uses ultrasound to measure ocean depth.

title: Heat and Temperature
content: Heat is a form of energy, measured in Joules or Calories. Temperature measures how hot/cold, in °C, °F, or Kelvin. Conduction: heat transfer through solids (metals are good conductors). Convection: heat transfer through liquids/gases (boiling water, wind). Radiation: heat transfer without medium (sunlight). Specific heat capacity: energy needed to raise 1kg by 1°C. Water has high specific heat (4186 J/kg°C). Latent heat: energy for phase change without temperature change.

title: Magnetism
content: Magnets have North and South poles. Like poles repel, unlike poles attract. Earth is a giant magnet with magnetic field. Magnetic field lines go from North to South outside magnet. Electromagnet: coil of wire with current creates magnetic field. Uses: electric motors, generators, MRI machines, speakers. Magnetic materials: iron, nickel, cobalt. Non-magnetic: wood, plastic, glass. Compass needle aligns with Earth's magnetic field.

title: Work, Energy and Power
content: Work = Force × Distance × cos(θ). SI unit: Joule (J). Energy is the capacity to do work. Types: Kinetic Energy (KE = ½mv²) and Potential Energy (PE = mgh). Law of Conservation of Energy: energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed. Power = Work/Time, measured in Watts (W). 1 horsepower = 746 W. Examples: falling ball converts PE to KE. Renewable energy: solar, wind, hydro. Non-renewable: coal, petroleum, natural gas.

title: Atoms and Molecules
content: Atom is the smallest unit of an element. Parts: protons (positive, in nucleus), neutrons (neutral, in nucleus), electrons (negative, orbiting). Atomic number = number of protons. Mass number = protons + neutrons. Molecule: two or more atoms bonded together. H₂O (water) = 2 hydrogen + 1 oxygen. Elements: substances with one type of atom (118 known). Compounds: two or more elements chemically combined. Isotopes: same element, different neutrons (Carbon-12, Carbon-14).

title: Periodic Table
content: Periodic table has 118 elements arranged by atomic number. 7 periods (horizontal rows) and 18 groups (vertical columns). Group 1: Alkali metals (Li, Na, K) - very reactive. Group 2: Alkaline earth metals (Be, Mg, Ca). Group 17: Halogens (F, Cl, Br) - reactive non-metals. Group 18: Noble gases (He, Ne, Ar) - inert/unreactive. Metals on left side, non-metals on right. Metalloids (B, Si, Ge) border between metals and non-metals. Mendeleev first organized elements by atomic mass.

title: Acids, Bases, and pH
content: Acids taste sour, turn blue litmus red, pH < 7. Examples: HCl (hydrochloric), H₂SO₄ (sulfuric), CH₃COOH (acetic/vinegar). Bases taste bitter, turn red litmus blue, pH > 7. Examples: NaOH (caustic soda), KOH, Ca(OH)₂ (lime water). pH 7 is neutral (pure water). pH scale: 0-14. Acid + Base → Salt + Water (neutralization). Indicators: litmus paper, phenolphthalein, methyl orange. Strong acids fully dissociate; weak acids partially dissociate. Stomach has HCl (pH 1.5-3.5). Antacids neutralize excess stomach acid.

title: Chemical Reactions
content: Chemical reaction: substances interact to form new substances. Types: Combination (A+B→AB), Decomposition (AB→A+B), Displacement (A+BC→AC+B), Double displacement (AB+CD→AD+CB), Combustion (fuel+O₂→CO₂+H₂O). Balanced equation: same atoms on both sides. Exothermic: releases heat (burning). Endothermic: absorbs heat (photosynthesis). Catalysts speed up reactions without being consumed. Rate factors: temperature, concentration, surface area, catalyst.

title: Carbon and Organic Chemistry
content: Carbon has 4 valence electrons, can form 4 bonds. Organic chemistry: chemistry of carbon compounds. Hydrocarbons: compounds of C and H only. Alkanes (single bonds): methane CH₄, ethane C₂H₆. Alkenes (double bond): ethylene C₂H₄. Alkynes (triple bond): acetylene C₂H₂. Isomers: same formula, different structure. Functional groups: -OH (alcohol), -COOH (carboxylic acid), -CHO (aldehyde). Carbon allotropes: diamond, graphite, fullerene, graphene.

title: Cell Structure and Function
content: Cell is the basic unit of life. Discovered by Robert Hooke (1665). Two types: Prokaryotic (no nucleus - bacteria) and Eukaryotic (with nucleus - plants, animals). Cell membrane: controls what enters/exits. Nucleus: contains DNA, controls cell activities. Mitochondria: powerhouse of cell, produces ATP energy. Ribosomes: make proteins. Endoplasmic reticulum: transport network. Golgi apparatus: packaging and shipping. Plant cells have: cell wall, chloroplasts, large vacuole. Animal cells: no cell wall, small vacuoles.

title: Photosynthesis
content: Photosynthesis: process by which plants make food using sunlight. Equation: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + sunlight → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Occurs in chloroplasts containing chlorophyll (green pigment). Light reaction: in thylakoids, water splits, O₂ released, ATP made. Dark reaction (Calvin cycle): in stroma, CO₂ fixed into glucose. Factors affecting: light intensity, CO₂ concentration, temperature, water. Plants are autotrophs (make own food). Photosynthesis is the basis of all food chains.

title: Human Digestive System
content: Digestion breaks food into nutrients. Mouth: teeth chew food, saliva contains amylase (breaks starch). Esophagus: carries food to stomach via peristalsis. Stomach: HCl and pepsin break proteins, churns food (2-4 hours). Small intestine (6m): bile from liver breaks fats, pancreatic juice, nutrients absorbed through villi. Large intestine: absorbs water, forms stool. Liver: largest internal organ, produces bile, detoxifies blood. Pancreas: produces insulin and digestive enzymes.

title: Human Respiratory System
content: Respiration: process of gas exchange - O₂ in, CO₂ out. Path: Nose → Pharynx → Larynx → Trachea → Bronchi → Bronchioles → Alveoli. Alveoli: tiny air sacs where gas exchange occurs. Diaphragm: muscle that controls breathing. Inhalation: diaphragm contracts, air enters. Exhalation: diaphragm relaxes, air exits. Lungs have ~300 million alveoli. Hemoglobin in RBCs carries O₂. Breathing rate: 12-20 breaths/minute at rest. Aerobic respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP.

title: Human Circulatory System
content: Heart: muscular organ with 4 chambers (2 atria, 2 ventricles). Pumps blood through body. Right side: deoxygenated blood to lungs. Left side: oxygenated blood to body. Arteries: carry blood AWAY from heart (thick walls, high pressure). Veins: carry blood TO heart (thin walls, valves). Capillaries: tiny vessels, gas/nutrient exchange. Blood: RBCs (carry O₂), WBCs (fight infection), Platelets (clotting), Plasma (liquid). Blood groups: A, B, AB (universal recipient), O (universal donor). Heart beats ~72 times/minute.

title: Genetics and Heredity
content: Genetics: study of inheritance. DNA (Deoxyribonucleic Acid): carries genetic information. Gene: segment of DNA coding for a protein/trait. Chromosome: DNA coiled around proteins. Humans have 46 chromosomes (23 pairs). XX = female, XY = male. Gregor Mendel: father of genetics, studied pea plants. Dominant trait: expressed even with one copy (Tt shows dominant). Recessive trait: expressed only with two copies (tt). Genotype: genetic makeup (TT, Tt, tt). Phenotype: physical expression (tall, short). Mutation: change in DNA sequence.

title: Evolution
content: Evolution: change in species over time through natural selection. Charles Darwin: proposed theory of evolution by natural selection (1859). Natural selection: organisms with favorable traits survive and reproduce more. Evidence: fossils, comparative anatomy, DNA similarities, embryology. Homologous organs: same origin, different function (human arm, whale flipper). Analogous organs: different origin, same function (bird wing, insect wing). Vestigial organs: reduced organs (appendix, wisdom teeth). Human evolution: Australopithecus → Homo habilis → Homo erectus → Homo sapiens.

title: Ecosystem and Environment
content: Ecosystem: community of living organisms interacting with their environment. Components: Biotic (living: plants, animals) and Abiotic (non-living: water, soil, air). Food chain: Producer → Primary consumer → Secondary consumer → Tertiary consumer. Food web: interconnected food chains. Energy flows: only 10% transfers to next trophic level. Decomposers break down dead matter (fungi, bacteria). Biodiversity: variety of life in an area. Threats: deforestation, pollution, climate change, overhunting. Conservation: national parks, wildlife sanctuaries, biosphere reserves.

title: Atoms and Nuclear Physics
content: Atom: smallest particle of an element. Nucleus contains protons and neutrons. Electrons orbit in shells. Radioactivity: unstable atoms emit radiation. Alpha (α): 2 protons + 2 neutrons. Beta (β): electron or positron. Gamma (γ): electromagnetic radiation. Half-life: time for half the atoms to decay. Nuclear fission: splitting heavy atom (nuclear power plants, atomic bomb). Nuclear fusion: combining light atoms (sun's energy, hydrogen bomb). E = mc² (Einstein): mass converts to enormous energy.

title: Solar System
content: Sun: star at center, made of hydrogen and helium. 8 planets: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars (rocky/inner), Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune (gas giants/outer). Mercury: smallest, closest to Sun. Venus: hottest, thick CO₂ atmosphere. Earth: only planet with life, liquid water. Mars: Red Planet, largest volcano (Olympus Mons). Jupiter: largest planet, Great Red Spot storm. Saturn: famous rings made of ice and rock. Moon: Earth's natural satellite, causes tides. Asteroids orbit between Mars and Jupiter. Comets: ice and rock, long tails near Sun.

title: Stars and Universe
content: Stars: massive balls of hot gas (hydrogen fusion). Sun is a medium-sized star. Star lifecycle: Nebula → Protostar → Main sequence → Red giant → White dwarf/Supernova → Neutron star/Black hole. Light year: distance light travels in 1 year (9.46 × 10¹² km). Nearest star: Proxima Centauri (4.24 light years). Milky Way: our galaxy with 100-400 billion stars. Universe: ~13.8 billion years old (Big Bang theory). Expanding universe discovered by Edwin Hubble.
